question: Hello, everyone! I would like to implement `child_archives` for the `QuantumEespresssoParser`, similar to how it's done for the GW section in the `ExcitingParser(BeyondDFTWorkflowsParser)`. To do this, I've been trying to understand how `child_archives` work in the `ExcitingParser`. However, there are some parts I find unclear. In particular, I don’t understand how the `_child_archives` dictionary is initialized. How are the correct keys determined?
answer: HI, the child archives can be generated by returning a list of strings in the `get_mainfile_keys` function. These will then be the keys to the archives in self._child_archives. The keys are completely dependent on you as long as they are different. For example, if your main entry demands that you create an extra worklfow entry to link it to other entries, then the `get_mainfile_keys` function can return `['workflow']`. If you want to dig deeper, the QE parser entry point in electronicparsers returns a MatchingParserInterface obj defined in nomad.parsing.parser. Here you find the `get_mainfile_keys` method wrapped in `is_mainfile` to generate the child archives. A bit of a disclaimer, you will find several inconsistencies there like why make child_archives a private variable and not directly passed to the parse function and why the need for this MatchingParserInterface class. There is some history explaining these details. In the new parsers, I am getting rid of this interface.

answer: Pielsticker I am wondering do you usually squash you commit before merging to the main or master branch? I see a long list of commit in the main branch. I think there is a bit of difference between the 2 use cases. Merge vs rebase: if you merge, you add an extra commit, while if you rebase you do not add an extra, but checnge them all. squash vs. all: here if you keep all commits, the commit history becomes long, but you can see all individually committed changes. In NOMAD, there is actually a convention that we indeed go for squash. In big projects, a too long commit history can cause performance issues, so this is why the option for squash has been introduced.

question: The case is ``` some_group(NXsome_baseclass_that_is_neither_collection_nor_object): UPPERCASE_ONE(NX_NUMBER): nameType: any doc: Something cool unit: NX_LENGTH UPPERCASE_TWO(NX_NUMBER): nameType: any doc: Also something cool, but different unit: NX_LENGTH ``` Should this be allowed? Well the docstring is not the same dimensions is not constrained why not?
answer: Whether it is `multiple-level single` or `multiple inheritance`, the inheritance we are following in NeXus does not justify any of them. Please note that NeXus Ontology v2 (what we implemented with Sherjeel) uses the NeXus definitions to allow ontological RDF declarations, like ``` <NXroot> <has> <NXentry> <NXem/ENTRY> <is_a> <NXentry> <NXem/ENTRY> <has> <NXem/ENTRY_start_time> <NXem/ENTRY_start_time> <has_value_container> <NX_DATE_TIME> <datafile> <type_of> <NXroot> <datafile> <has> <datafile/entry1> <datafile/entry1> <type_of> <NXem/ENTRY> <datafile/entry1> <has> <datafile/entry1/start_time> <datafile/entry1/start_time> <type_of> <NXem/ENTRY/start_time> <datafile/entry1/start_time> <hasValueContainer> <datafile/entry1/start_time/value> <datafile/entry1/start_time/value> <type_of> <NX_DATE_TIME> <datafile/entry1/start_time/value> <actualValue> <20250101> ``` The question is if we like this language which uses only a few verbs in its SVO (subject-verb-object) topology. Or do we want to extend its vocabulary of verbs (namely the object properties)? This could also mean that we could reduce the object vocabulary (namely the concepts), and would not necessarily differentiate anymore between `NXem/ENTRY/start_time`, `NXentry_start_time`, or just simply `start_time`, but rather use a single concept for all. We could than have statements like: ``` <NXroot> <has (or contains)> <NXentry> <NXentry> <has_start_time(or started_at)> <NX_DATE_TIME> <datafile> <type_of> <NXroot> <datafile> <contains> <datafile/entry1> <datafile/entry1> <type_of> <NXentry> <datafile/entry1> <started_at> <20250101> ``` You can see that the NeXus Fields are not converted here to Subjects, but to Verbs. This language is more English like (not using long constructed words for specialised terms, like "NXem/ENTRY/start_time" as German speakers would do), and uses very generic terms which must be put in context and the context is specialising them to their actual meaning. This means that all the possible specialisations (e.g. of any detector) are actually flattened into a very generic concept `DETECTOR`. This is exactly what NeXus assumed and this is how NeXus Ontology v1 was (half-)implemented. Next Tuesday, we will have a meeting about it at 11 am ion our OSCARS room. All of you being interested are welcome.

question: But how do I then Update my Images? I would then Need to do a Version Release for each Plugin for every Minor change correct? The Reason I deleted the lock files was that I always got conflicts with the Plugins otherwise
answer: This is my current best understanding of how updating a plugin version should be done (maybe @ahmed ilyas can correct my if something is wrong in my explanation): - In your `nomad-distro-template` you update the plugin to some specific version using `pyproject.toml`. This version can come from a github branch, github commit, github tag, PyPI, custom pip registry etc. - When you push these changes to `nomad-distro-template`, there is an automatic pipeline that generates the uv lockfile and lets you know if there are dependency conflicts. - In case of dependency conflicts, there are several ways to deal with them: - You simply ignore the fact that there is a conflict and manually ask UV to use override to using some specific version. This should be possible with [uv override-dependencies](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/reference/settings/#override-dependencies) - If you know that the dependency conflict is serious, then you will need to get it fixed in one of the packages that produces the conflict. If jupyterhub > 5.0 is not working with the NORTH tools of 1.3.16, you cannot update to it. But then also we need to add a version restriction into 1.3.16 that will already tell you that during the installation phase so that you don't only realize it after trying to run it.

question: All 🙂 I experienced the same hurdle with zip files as @Klaus Götz in his [post](https://discord.com/channels/1201445470485106719/1205480348050395136/1302979204941615175): Is there any possibility to avoid the automatic unpacking of .zip files if one uploads them als ELN annotations (Oasis v1.3.14) with ``` processed_data_as_zip_file = Quantity( type=str, description="A reference to an uploaded .zip archive of processed data containing plain x-y-value table as .txt files.", a_browser={ "adaptor": "RawFileAdaptor"}, a_eln={"component": "FileEditQuantity", "label": "Processed data as zip archive"}, ) ``` It's the same reason: the user uploads some packed processed data files (to allow multiple file uploads in one run instead of many clicks), which will be then 'processed/normalized' and plotted altogether. Alternatively, if the unpacking can not be prevented, is there any option to at least 'know/get' the filename list inside the zip?
answer: Klaus and ron, Unfortunately nomad automatically extracts compressed files and right now there is no way to change this behavior. This step is triggered prior to any parsing step, so maybe better to have a parser to deal with the content of the extracted files. But in general, we probably need to consider in future releases (@laurih8700). That zip-inside-zip trick works but im afraid on reprocessing the upload, any top most compressed files will get extracted again (need to check to confirm) There are also many image collections circulating in public repositories where proper parsers provided where I even see substantiated arguments as that this content can essentially always stay compressed in a zip file. For another example, e.g. HDF5 with default compression filter deflate used nowadays h5py allows working with such data out of the box where interestingly where it is then the lower-levels of the library that does the decompression and compression for the users.

answer: can be changed in nomad config You have to upgrade the `upload_limit` in nomad.yaml fle: https://nomad-lab.eu/prod/v1/staging/docs/reference/config.html Kuehbach just wanted to make you aware that in the NIAC repo, the following PR will soon be merged: https://github.com/nexusformat/definitions/pull/1507/files#diff-a8ea69b8761124d034b9fe39b97f27dd70091ed2f9b4772bce000def7f7f51a1R49. This explicitly implements multiple rules that were so far only given implicitly in the manual. It adds groups and fields to `NXobject` that are supposed to be used everywhere and thus shall be inherited from `NXobject`. What I wanted to highlight here is that this includes the reserved suffixes (search for `Reserved suffixes` in https://manual.nexusformat.org/datarules.html). Specifically, this also includes the field `FIELDNAME_set`, to be used to give target values for another field called `FIELDNAME`. I think this will be problematic with all the base classes that you implemented that also end on `_set`, see https://fairmat-nfdi.github.io/nexus_definitions/search.html?q=_set. I don't think it is a good idea to overwrite the field `FIELDNAME_set` (inherited from NXobject) with a group like `NXcoordinate_system_set`. What's your opinion on this? `_set` was reserved for a long time, so I think we will have to adapt.

question: I have a very general conceptual question about data description. I'm the author of the PyMoDAQ data acquisition framework. Most of the acquired data there are actually multi-dimensional, for instance a spectrum as a function of one or more varying parameters. Hence the data array rank could be 1, 2, 3, ...N I standardized this data representation using the notion of navigation axes (the ones attached to the varying parameters: power, polarization, XY position of a sample...) and signal axes (the ones attached to a detector: wavelength for instance). The (python) objects representing the data, the corresponding hdf5 saving and the graphical representation is mature and standardized ([pymodaq_data](https://pymodaq.cnrs.fr/en/5.0.x_dev/data_management.html)). One feature of pymodaq is to allow a user to quickly go from one type of measurement to another (just what you need to properly prepare your setup). And then explore their data in the corresponding hdf5 file (regenerating python object at will if needed). This adaptable way is however very difficult to fit with the Nexus way that has been developed around "macro" detectors. PyMoDAQ is more oriented around the object of study and the association of various base instruments/actuators and scans using them. Have you thought about such issue and what would you propose as a possible way?
answer: seb5g, NXdata is not per se coupled to a detector. Instead, please have a look into NXdata which based on what you describe covers several of your use cases. Instances of data arrays in NXdata can have dimensionality > 3, more details here: https://github.com/nexusformat/definitions/blob/main/base_classes/NXdata.nxdl.xml I point here to the latest version as this is the one that will be pulled into the FAIRmat-specific customizations of NeXus. There was a meeting with the NIAC (NeXus International Advisory Committee with whom we discussed most of our latest proposals for changes in NeXus, many of these proposals have been accepted and are currently in a process of becoming merged into the official NeXus branch. There are NXDL schemas for microscopy specifically https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/nexus_definitions/blob/fairmat/contributed_definitions/NXimage_set.nxdl.xml https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/nexus_definitions/blob/fairmat/contributed_definitions/NXspectrum_set.nxdl.xml Feel free to have a look, my feeling though you are currently in a process of assessing whether to consider using NeXus (in addition to or instead of either way) I think your questions are more detailed then I have time typing here, maybe it is more efficient to bring them to one of the Area Task Force meetings or to schedule a specific meeting tailored to your questions. I on parental leave until 31.12. this year, therefore my time is limited. A little bit contextualization about specific usage examples of your framework that you currently plan to use NeXus on would be useful. Feel free to contact @Sandor Brockhauser to arrange a meeting if the above material is insufficient to answer your questions. I think the case you are running into right now is how to find a compromise between convenience/ease for users and have things in NeXus. Well, an application definition is a contract between a data producer and a data consumer. Which means what should the contract contain. NeXus encodes the what it contains. So if your framework writes out plain multidimensional arrays (multiple of them) and allow people to flexibly combine all good but think about the perspective of an application/scientific software that should now consume this adaptive content. Point is the communication of an xy plot as an example requires data, the x and y axis, i.e. these are required other possibly relevant axis do not need to be required. NeXus allows you to specify for which concepts you have instance data and which constraints (data type, shape, dimensionality, existence constraints) you expect for these. That's all from my side, as I am on parental leave I will not now make this a live conversation but please drop your comments to be read and answered asynchronously. Thank you

question: There should not be technical problem with this a nest of instruments in one another. I have three suggestions against doing this and substantiation why though, personal thoughts: 1.) Write "\*_instrument" instead of "\*_instruments" replace star with stm and afm, plural here is not optimal as there can only be one such node with the name (NXinstrument)/stm_instruments not multiple unless you add at least some ALLCAPITAL postfix to the symbol of these concepts 2.) The situation you are building is the same like I couple an atom probe instrument to an electron microscope (e.g. the TOMO in Jülich) I would not do it like this but rather couple the two application definitions NXapm and NXem at a higher level of the hierarchy i.e in your case NXstm and NXafm 3.) NXinstrument originally means "the instrument and the lab in which it stands" that being said why at all to nest instruments into one another?
answer: I guess the plan is anyway to have NXstpm as the "umbrella" appdef that then gets extended/specizalized by NXTsm, NXafm, and so on, right? In that case, I would do it like this: ```yaml NXspm(NXobject): doc: | This is the generic appdef for SPM experiments, including STM, AFM, STS, etc. (NXinstrument): # Here, you put instrument parts/metadata that are common for all SPM experiments. name: description: (NXfabrication): tip_material: [...] ``` and then for the extending appdefs: ```yaml NXstm(NXspm): (NXinstrument): # Here you put all STM instrument metadata, specializing NXspm/NXinstrument. NXsafm(NXspm): (NXinstrument): doc: | # Here you put all AFM instrument metadata, specializing NXspm/NXinstrument. ``` Thus, you only need to maintain the common metadata once. I think @Markus Kuehbach's example is different in that you have a setup which both has an AFM and a STM. In that case, I would indeed write separate entries for the AFM (with NXafm) and STM data (using NXstm).

question: For anyone using VSCode: are the Ruff format rules being applied when you save? I have the option `"editor.formatOnSave": true,` in the `settings.json`, but for some reason, Ruff is not applying on saving a file
answer: Daelman you need some lines in the toml file of your package: ``` [project.optional-dependencies] dev = ["ruff", "pytest", "structlog"] [tool.ruff] # Exclude a variety of commonly ignored directories. exclude = [ ".bzr", ".direnv", ".eggs", ".git", ".git-rewrite", ".hg", ".mypy_cache", ".nox", ".pants.d", ".pytype", ".ruff_cache", ".svn", ".tox", ".venv", "__pypackages__", "_build", "buck-out", "build", "dist", "node_modules", "venv", ] # Same as Black. line-length = 88 indent-width = 4 [tool.ruff.lint] select = [ # pycodestyle "E", # Pyflakes "F", # pyupgrade "UP", # isort "I", # pylint "PL", ] ignore = [ "F403", # 'from module import *' used; unable to detect undefined names ] fixable = ["ALL"] # Allow unused variables when underscore-prefixed. dummy-variable-rgx = "^(_+|(_+[a-zA-Z0-9_]*[a-zA-Z0-9]+?))$" # this is entirely optional, you can remove this if you wish to [tool.ruff.format] # use single quotes for strings. quote-style = "single" # indent with spaces, rather than tabs. indent-style = "space" # Like Black, respect magic trailing commas. skip-magic-trailing-comma = false # Like Black, automatically detect the appropriate line ending. line-ending = "auto" ``` https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/nomad-plugin-template

question: hey, one question, is there something which matches a CIF file to a nomad results section in nomad?
answer: ```python def my_normalization(self, archive: 'EntryArchive', logger: 'BoundLogger') -> None: if self.cif_files is not None: # Read the CIF files and convert them into ase atoms ase_atoms_list = [] for cif_file in self.cif_files: with archive.m_context.raw_file(cif_file) as file: ase_atoms_list.append(read(file.name)) print(ase_atoms_list) # Let's save the composition and structure into archive.results.material if not archive.results.material: archive.results.material = Material() # populate elemets from a set aof all the elemsts in ase_atoms elements = set() for ase_atoms in ase_atoms_list: elements.update(ase_atoms.get_chemical_symbols()) archive.results.material.elements = list(elements) # Create a System: this is a NOMAD specific data structure for storing structural # and chemical information that is suitable for both experiments and simulations. topology = {} for ase_atoms in ase_atoms_list: system = System( atoms=nomad_atoms_from_ase_atoms(ase_atoms), label='Auto-XRD reference', description='Reference structure used to train the auto-XRD model.', structural_type='bulk', dimensionality='3D', ) add_system_info(system, topology) add_system(system, topology) archive.results.material.topology = list(topology.values()) ``` I think this could be something that could go to `nomad.normalizing.common` with options to use spglib or pymatgen to also parse the symmetry. Any thoughts @Lauri Himanen? Would be super handy for many things We should not have these kind of tools in the nomad core package. We have just spent a great deal of effort to get rid of such dependencies. Could instead be some plugin that includes these tools.

question: What is the EnergyContribution def? The one in `energies.py`?
answer: ```python class BaseEnergy(PhysicalProperty): """ Abstract class used to define a common `value` quantity with the appropriate units for different types of energies, which avoids repeating the definitions for each energy class. """ value = Quantity( type=np.float64, unit='joule', description=""" """, ) def normalize(self, archive: 'EntryArchive', logger: 'BoundLogger') -> None: super().normalize(archive, logger) class EnergyContribution(BaseEnergy, PropertyContribution): """ Abstract class for incorporating specific energy contributions to the `TotalEnergy`. The inheritance from `PropertyContribution` allows to link this contribution to a specific component (of class `BaseModelMethod`) of the over `ModelMethod` using the `model_method_ref` quantity. For example, for a force field calculation, the `model_method_ref` may point to a particular potential type (e.g., a Lennard-Jones potential between atom types X and Y), while for a DFT calculation, it may point to a particular electronic interaction term (e.g., 'XC' for the exchange-correlation term, or 'Hartree' for the Hartree term). Then, the contribution will be named according to this model component and the `value` quantity will contain the energy contribution from this component evaluated over all relevant atoms or electrons or as a function of them. """ # TODO address the dual parent normalization explicity def normalize(self, archive: 'EntryArchive', logger: 'BoundLogger') -> None: super().normalize(archive, logger) ``` Oh maybe it's because of this dual inheritance, you mentioned that to me ```python class PropertyContribution(PhysicalProperty): """ Abstract physical property section linking a property contribution to a contribution from some method. Abstract class for incorporating specific contributions of a physical property, while linking this contribution to a specific component (of class `BaseModelMethod`) of the over `ModelMethod` using the `model_method_ref` quantity. """ model_method_ref = Quantity( type=BaseModelMethod, description=""" Reference to the `ModelMethod` section to which the property is linked to. """, a_eln=ELNAnnotation(component='ReferenceEditQuantity'), ) def normalize(self, archive, logger) -> None: super().normalize(archive, logger) if not self.name: self.name = self.get('model_method_ref').get('name') ```

answer: > We have to talk a couple of topics there, on to where this should go (NumericalSettings or AtomsState? Or maybe it is a numerical settings with references to the atoms state?) question: ` try: if hasattr(entry, "definition__field") and entry.definition__field: # Directly use entry.definition__field as class_name class_name = entry.definition__field.strip() # Fetch superclasses from the server response = requests.get(f"http://localhost:8089/superclasses/{class_name}") if response.status_code == 200: superclasses = response.json().get("superclasses", []) if archive.results.eln.methods is None: archive.results.eln.methods = [] for superclass in superclasses: if superclass not in archive.results.eln.methods: archive.results.eln.methods.append(superclass) else: logger.warning( f"Failed to fetch superclasses: {response.status_code} - {response.text}" ) else: logger.warning("entry.definition__field is missing or empty.") archive.results.eln.methods.append("Generic Experiment") except Exception as e: logger.warning(f"Failed to extract superclasses: {e}") archive.results.eln.methods.append("Generic Experiment")` the two else and exxept?
answer: the point, but if we are already inside the `if hasattr(entry, "definition__field") and entry.definition__field` if clause we know that this is not just a generic experiment. So, if the service fails (i.e., `response.status_code != 200`), we can have the warning `f"Failed to fetch superclasses: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"`, but why not add the definition name to archive.eln.methods still? The definitions are a controlled vocabulary already. So I suggest we remove the ``` if self.method and self.method not in archive.results.eln.methods: archive.results.eln.methods.append(self.method) elif self.m_def.name not in archive.results.eln.methods archive.results.eln.methods.append(self.m_def.name) ``` part as you suggested, but instead in your code, we add line like this: ``` else: logger.warning( f"Failed to fetch superclasses: {response.status_code} - {response.text}" ) archive.results.ln.methods.append(class_name) ``` Then we get the name of the application definition even if the service fails. That's then essentially the class without its superclasses. It somehow still has a different set of definitions compared to master. This is not intentional, right? Rebasing is tricky when you have submodules. If you are fine to use the definitions at master, can you run from the command line ``` git submodule sync --recursive git submodule update --init --recursive --remote --jobs=4 git submodule foreach --recursive 'git fetch --tags' cd src/pynxtools/definitions printf "$(git describe --dirty --tags --long --abbrev=8 --match '*[0-9]*')" > ../nexus-version.txt ``` That should set the definitions to the latest, which is also what we have in the master branch Also, you'll need to add owlready2, fastapi, and pygit2 to the pyproject.toml file under dependencies

question: Some feedback on this proposal would be welcomed: https://github.com/nomad-coe/nomad-simulations/issues/158?issue=nomad-coe%7Cnomad-simulations%7C242
answer: i did not understand, what is a new `model_system` ? why doesnt it go to somewhere along the hierarchy level of `Cell` ? What you are talking about is strictly crystallography-centric..... **In terms of the schema:** 1) Wyckoff positions are only meaningful in the context of periodic crystals with a space group and a conventional cell. For molecules, amorphous stuff, surfaces, etc., the concept doesn’t apply. So adding them as a general hierarchy alongside atomic positions feels super inconsistent and unintuitive from my side **In terms of science:** 2)Mimicking “atomic positions” with a separate Wyckoff hierarchy inside of the `ModelSystem` is misleading: Wyckoff sites are not independent coordinates but a symmetry classification of the atomic positions. They’re literally the meta-data. It makes more sense to have it as an auxiliary metadata attached to the crystal system like `Symmetry` or `Topology`, not as a parallel `model_system`

answer: Sorry this is the real version of my file This should not actually be a problem of the parsing of nxs files in NOMAD, we don't have any special rules in place for `NXcollection` (it is is only considered special in the validation) The problem is related to the linking. Essentially, in `/NXafm/ENTRY/reproducibility_indicators`, we only define `cantilever_oscillator`, but none of its sub-items. NOMAD will then only try to parse the `cantilever_oscillator` group from the link, but not anything that is inside that Hdf5 group. This is the general case for links, if you want to have all sub-elements also show up at the link destination, you need to explicitly define them in application definition as well.

question: Daelman @hyllios is there any mapping available (either in NOMAD or libxc) specifying: ```python "<jacobs_ladder_type>": <list_of_libxc_labels> ``` ? I am looking at https://www.tddft.org/programs/libxc/functionals/, so it will be ideal ot have this mapping like e.g.: ```python libxc_map = { "LDA exchange": ["LDA_X", "LDA_X_1D_EXPONENTIAL", ...], "LDA correlation": ["LDA_C_1D_CSC", "", ...] ..
answer: Yes, my answer above covers the extraction of `DFT.jacobs_ladder`, but you can just merge and I'll add the normalization there. Some feedback on this schema suggestion: 1. Since we're flattening everything out, I would just store the `XCFunctional` quantities directly in DFT, no need for a subsection. TDDFT can inherit from DFT itself. Other DFT-derived methods might too,. I'll have to look into that. 2. `XCFunctional.name` is a misnomer. This is the `kind` of physics targeted by the functional, its name comes after. 3. `self_interaction_correction_method` is also a zoo, not sure if we'll get around to exhaustively covering all of it. Also take a look at my next point. 4. While implementing this kind of extraction is fine for now, I would move to adding more of this knowledge dynamically at the search level. I've been looking into this with Lauri. While the whole reasoning is a bit too verbose to lay out, the idea is that these quantities more like *meta-meta-data* about the functional... To curate this more holistically and flexibly, it should be applied at another layer in the API, not storage. Ow, BIG point: `libxc_name` can also be set by the parser itself. In that case it should NOT be overwritten Regarding point 4, just the functional name, code and version are sufficient to retrieve all information. Adding `xc`, is like explaining what `tight_binding` means, rather than taking down the relevant orbitals at play. Don't get me wrong, this is fine in and of itself: we want to make this kind of data accessible to everyone. It just shouldn't be done inside the archive. Also, almost all DFT is `xc`: when people say the functional's name in a calculation, they're referring to `xc` . Almost no code allows you to choose just `c` or `x`. That distinction really just comes from `lib_xc` to allow for flexibility.

question: Is one more preferable than the other? ```py '../uploads/{upload_id}/raw/{mainfile}#/data' '../uploads/{upload_id}/archive/{entry_id}#/data' ```
answer: I think the purpose is different, does the first one actually works when rfcing data? From the API dashboard: https://nomad-lab.eu/prod/v1/staging/api/v1/extensions/docs#/uploads%2Fraw/get_upload_raw_path_uploads__upload_id__raw__path__get -> gets the raw file on path (that's why is confusing you can point to data https://nomad-lab.eu/prod/v1/staging/api/v1/extensions/docs#/uploads%2Farchive/get_upload_entry_archive_uploads__upload_id__archive__entry_id__get -> this one gets the archive, so it makes sense if you can further specify sections underneath In any case, there are situations where one single `mainfile` will be generating more than one entry (hence multiple entry_id), so I'd say the second option is safer. question: How r u coupling photons in the new-schema BSE workflow?
answer: I am not 100% we should go into such fine details ourselves. What is true, is that the `Photon` class should be reworked, but for it, we can just work it out before releasing a version 1.0 of the data schema For BSE: - Each Photon direction is considered a separate entry with a section `method.photon`. - The BSE entry contains all 3 Photon entries, and generates a new section `method.bse`. - There is another layer of complexity, the `XS` workflow: this one is the one handling `DFT+GW+BSE`

question: But does that line in pyproject.toml make pynxtools available like this or is it more like nomad.something.nexus.dataconverter? I'll quickly install the nomad package in a fresh env and see what happens
answer: To my understanding, with `[tool.setuptools.packages.find]` it goes to the pynxtools package at `nomad.dependencies.parsers.nexus` and also checks the `[tool.setuptools.packages.find]` of the pyproject.toml file of THAT package to install the packages defined there. Thus, you can directly install from `pynxtools.dataconverter`. > Do you know if we can choose [all] somehow from package.find? I don't think that will work because the plugins are their own packages and not actually submodules of pynxtools. Essentially, they are their own packages that should be installed additionally. The best would be to install pynxtools[all] as dependency in nomad, as suggested by Florian in this MR: https://gitlab.mpcdf.mpg.de/nomad-lab/nomad-FAIR/-/merge_requests/1629. Then, one could even install just one of the plugins in your local OASIS by overwriting in the pyproject.toml, e.g. pynxtools[mpes]. However, since this MR is currently breaking the tests and in general not working, the only workaround that I can think of is to add a manual install of pynxtools to the Dockerfile as we had it before: ``` WORKDIR /install/dependencies/parsers/nexus RUN pip install -r requirements.txt RUN pip install --no-deps ".[all]" WORKDIR /install ``` Yeah, the submodule commit of pynxtools that we are sitting on currently is still before we did the pluginization, at least of the MPES/XPS readers. The STM/STS reader is however not available on NOMAD (at least as part of the NexusDataConverter on the central nomad) since it is anyway a plugin. My main issue is that whatever I change in the pynxtools-xps reader is not going to show up in the central nomad any longer. So if I make an error fix for the SPECS reader for example, the people from that company won't be able to use it on Nomad, not even in a special dev deployment. They are not going to install an OASIS, they just want to play around with the data conversion in NOMAD. I can always let them use my local OASIS, but eventually, things should be available through the central NOMAD as well.

question: Who is supposed to do this based on which procedure? This pynxtools release should use the newest version of the nx fairmat branch possible
answer: I think if we update pynxtools to work with the latest definitions, the tests in pynxtools would break because there is still code that is not yet pluginized (specifically, the EM reader) which does not work with the latest definitions and thus would break upon updating the submodule, right? So I suggest you first move out all of the code to plugins that does not yet work with the newest defs and then we update the definitions. You can always create a branch in pynxtools with the latest commit submoduled and then in your plugin, you just install pynxtools from that commit And then once it works with your specific plugin, we merge the pynxtools with the latest defs and make a new release
